blocks.body: Pronouns and Demonstratives — Main pronoun and demonstrative groups.

• Personal / Object Pronouns have their own lesson: I/me, she/her, they/them.
  • This is the base for understanding subject and object roles in a sentence.
  • Example: She called me.
  • From here, move to possessive and reflexive patterns.
blocks.heading: Personal and object pronouns

blocks.body:
• Possessive adjectives vs pronouns have their own lesson: my book / the book is mine.
  • You learn when the form goes before a noun and when it replaces the noun completely.
  • Example: This is my bag. This bag is mine.
  • The same idea prepares you for one / ones as noun replacements.
blocks.heading: Possessive forms

blocks.body:
• Reflexive Pronouns have their own lesson: myself, yourself, themselves.
  • This topic also includes lessons on indefinite pronouns, relative pronouns, each / every / all and reciprocal pronouns.
  • These forms help you avoid repetition, make references clear and build natural sentences.
blocks.heading: Reflexive and related pronouns

blocks.body:
TopicLevelDescription
PronounsA1–A2A system overview and basic demonstratives
Personal / Object PronounsA1Subject and object forms
Possessive Adjectives vs PronounsA1–A2Pairs such as my / mine and your / yours
Reflexive PronounsA2–B1myself, yourself, themselves
Indefinite PronounsA2–B1someone, anyone, nobody and related forms
Relative PronounsB1who, which, that, whose
Each / Every / AllA2–B1Close determiners with different meanings
Reciprocal PronounsA2–B1each other, one another
One / OnesA2Replacing a noun without repeating it
blocks.heading: Topics in this guide

blocks.body:
• This / that / these / those point to distance, closeness or a specific choice.
  • The Pronouns overview lesson places them next to the other core pronoun groups.
  • That makes it easier to see how pronouns replace nouns and keep sentences clean.
blocks.heading: Demonstratives

blocks.body:
🧠

Start with Pronouns overview, then move to Personal / Object Pronouns and Possessive Adjectives vs Pronouns.

Study reflexive, indefinite and relative pronouns as separate steps: each group has its own patterns.

Each / Every / All, Reciprocal Pronouns and One / Ones close common confusion points around A2–B1.
blocks.heading: Study tips
